author: @NorthernKitty
text: I had never considered the butyl rubber approach!!  Very ingenious!  I have smaller speakers, so I usually just use any kind of pump bulb I have handy, like a turkey baster with the pipe portion removed, etc.  Squeeze the air out of the bulb, place the mouth of the bulb near the dent, then quickly unsqueeze the bulb to create some suction.  When using a vacuum cleaner, I think it might help to use a porous attachment like a brush or drape attachment to reduce your risk of damaging the cone.  Great job, Luke!! 😊

author: @malcolmwright7440
text: True story! Back in the 70’s I was sent to Musgroves, Perth, WA. by my new boss Les Lloyd of Thorne, to measure a set of Wharfedale speakers; we had on apro and display, so as to order new cones from our factory in Leichhardt as these were damaged by customers. When I walked in to Musgroves the sales staff were all talking behind the front desks and ignored me, and as I was on a parking meter which had only 30 mins parking, I quickly found the speakers on the raised stage and the voice coil dust covers were pushed in like your story. So I thought stuff this, I am not going to re-cone these for that, so I put my lips arround the dust covers and “sucked all four of them out”, woofers and tweeters, then used my handkerchief to wipe off my saliver and rushed back to my car before the meter expired. When I got back to Thorne in MtLawley the two girls behind our front counter were in tears and so was the boss, I could see him crying in his office while on the phone. The girls said Les wanted to see me urgently and I immediately thought one of my family had died and they had received the bad news. As my boss saw me he put the phone on his desk and came out of his office and approached me. It was then I realised he was not crying but was in tears laughing, he said that Bill McVee from Musgroves was on the phone telling him not to send anyone out because some stranger had just walked in and had “sucked off the Wharfedales”. He knew it was me from the description of the Samsonite attaché style tool case I was carrying; it looked like a business man's brief case and; between bouts of laughter, gave me specific instructions next time to introduce myself to the clients first. The girls behind our front desk thought it was the best thing ever. Wish I had kept the business cards they typed up for me “Can suck, Will travel”.
